title: SMSF investors go global after Aussie property ban
full_text:
The Albanese government’s ban on residential property lending inside self-managed super funds has done little to dampen the ambitions of Australia’s most determined property investors.
Many thought this would mark the end of property in super but it has instead redirected their focus toward opportunities that many had never seriously considered, from commercial assets on home soil to residential and commercial property in markets offshore.
Commercial property has been touted as the new “go to” option for SMSFs given the ability to borrow against them; however, another contender is emerging further afield where SMSF trustees can access lower price points and purchase in cash.
The enthusiasm for investing in property using an SMSF is clear in the numbers. Approximately a quarter of SMSF assets are invested in property, compared with the average industry super fund, which typically invests 7-12 per cent of assets in property.
But less than 2 per cent of SMSF assets are invested in overseas property, and this has remained stable over the past five years. But property experts are predicting the budget’s tax changes to drive a shift to offshore investments.
Kitty Parker, founder of buyers agency firm Kitty & Miles, has acted quickly, and recently purchased several New Zealand property assets including a commercial property for $NZ80,000 ($66,500) with a 23 per cent gross rental yield.
“NZ provides a stable market for property investors with a low-entry price point. When you consider the strong Aussie dollar and no stamp duty, it’s cost-effective compared to buying in Australia,” Ms Parker says.
“As with any property purchase, choosing the correct location and a quality property makes all the difference. For $200,000, SMSF trustees can get themselves a neat 1-2-bedroom residential unit with a gross rental return of 9 per cent.”
For someone with $300,000 to invest in property with their super fund, you can buy a ski-country house in Japan, a colonial mansion in Mexico, several sea-view flats in Georgia, or a beautiful villa in Sicily.
What you need to know
Although these may sound interesting, there is a lot more to work through when buying property overseas.
Firstly, you need to make sure your super fund trust deed and investment strategy permit overseas property investments. Next is to ensure the property will comply with superannuation rules. It may seem like a good idea to purchase a property in an overseas location that has personal relevance, such as a family home town.
After all, your family may be able to keep an eye on the property. But if you go down this pathway, be aware of the related party rules, which mean that relatives cannot rent out the property unless it is a commercial property.
And if you plan to spend time in the property after you retire, you would need to transfer the property out of the super fund into your own name to avoid breaching another rule – the sole purpose test.
Next is to figure out where to buy. As noted earlier, $300,000 can buy you a lot overseas but where do you focus?
Is it a two-bedroom villa in Bali that can be rented out short-term with a circa 10 per cent gross rental yield? Or is it a restored cottage in Portugal complete with olive trees and stable long-term rental prospects?
If the Australian dollar moves towards 80 US cents, the US might also be on the radar, as many states provide affordable property with high single-digit rental returns.
Moving to the question of how to buy, this is just as important as where to buy. Ms Parker notes that every country has its own nuances when it comes to negotiating and purchasing property, so a buyer’s agent should be used to safeguard against getting caught out.
“Not only do you need to be careful about country-specific rules, prospective buyers also need to be discerning regarding which city they buy in as well as the type of property,” she says.
“New Zealand, for example, has several popular locations for property investors that each have their own unique market drivers. As such, capital growth can differ dramatically, so do not think that simply deciding to buy in a country is enough, you need to find the right city within the right country.”
SMSF property ban – an extra Budget sting for investors
Post purchase, the usual hassles of owning an investment property apply, but on a magnified scale because the property is overseas. You may be dealing with real estate agents and tradespeople in different time zones with different currencies, and potentially communicating in different languages. Not to mention the differing rules that apply with regard to tenant rights and local property taxes.
Residential ban increases risks
But regardless of the potential pitfalls, risks and hassles, SMSF trustees will be forced to look further afield into places they would not normally look due to the incoming residential property lending ban.
It points to a situation where more risk is going to be taken due to the lack of familiarity, and potentially more property losses being realised.
If you are thinking about buying an investment property in super, seek professional advice from a buyer’s agent and ensure they have your best interests in mind. Many agents promote overseas property investment and try to entice you to purchase an overpriced off-the-plan property in an overdeveloped area with little capital growth prospects. Tread with caution.
James Gerrard is principal and director of financial planning firm www.financialadvisor.com.au
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
